title.title: Limitations & Risks
title.subtitle: An honest look at the oracle problem, real limitations, and where smart contracts fall short

oracle.heading: The Oracle Problem
oracle.subtitle: Smart contracts are closed and deterministic — reaching the outside world is the hard part.

cards.label: The Problem
cards.body: A contract can't fetch a price, score or delivery status itself — an external call would return different results on different nodes and break consensus.
cards.tag: The chain knows nothing outside itself

cards.label: The Fix — Oracles
cards.body: An off-chain service fetches the data and submits it as a signed transaction. The contract then reads it like any other on-chain value. Chainlink is the dominant network.

cards.label: The Catch
cards.body: A single controlled or hacked oracle becomes the point of manipulation — and can drain every contract that depends on it.
cards.tag: Only as decentralised as its weakest data source

example.label: Example — parametric crop insurance
example.bodyA: Premium paid → contract holds "if rainfall < X by date Z → pay $5k" →
example.bodyB: posts daily rainfall → condition met → payout fires automatically. No claims adjuster — but a broken oracle stalls everything.

challenges.heading: Challenges & Limitations
challenges.subtitle: Smart contracts inherit their chain's constraints — four that shape every design decision.

items.title: Throughput
items.what: L1 can't match Web2 volume
items.why: Consensus is the bottleneck — every node re-executes every transaction, so raw L1 throughput stays low.
items.fix: L2 rollups push it to thousands of TPS without giving up L1 security.

items.title: Latency
items.what: Block times vs millisecond web
items.why: A tx waits for the next block and several confirmations before it is safe to rely on.
items.fix: Use an L2 for instant UX; settle on L1 underneath.
items.stat: ETH block 12s · BTC ~10 min · L2 ~250 ms

items.title: Storage Cost
items.what: On-chain bytes are extraordinarily pricey
items.why: Every full node stores it forever, so the protocol prices storage to discourage bloat.
items.fix: Keep only a hash on-chain; put the data on IPFS / Arweave.

items.title: Computation Limits
items.what: Gas caps what a tx can compute
items.why: Each block has a gas ceiling, so ML inference or heavy loops simply can't run on-chain.
items.fix: Compute off-chain, post the result + a ZK proof the chain can verify.

technical.heading: Technical Challenges & Limitations
technical.subtitle: Four structural problems — what they are, why they happen, and how the field is fixing them.

items.title: MEV — Maximal Extractable Value
items.what: Block producers reorder transactions for profit
items.why: They decide which txs go in and in what order — so they can front-run or sandwich a trade they see waiting in the mempool.
items.fix: Private mempools, commit-reveal, MEV-aware DEXs (CoW Protocol, Flashbots SUAVE).

items.title: Upgradability
items.what: Immutable code can't be patched
items.why: Deployed code is permanent. A critical bug stays exploitable forever — The DAO lost $60M and forced an Ethereum hard fork.
items.fix: Audit before deploy; add upgrade proxies only when essential, behind a timelock + multisig.
items.stat: Proxy admin key = a centralisation point

items.title: The Blockchain Trilemma
items.what: Security · scalability · decentralisation — pick 2
items.why: Maximising all three at L1 isn't possible; every chain deliberately trades one away.
items.fix: Ethereum keeps security + decentralisation and pushes scale to L2 rollups.

items.title: State Growth
items.what: The chain grows ~50 GB/year — forever
items.why: History is append-only and every full node must keep all of it, so eventually only datacenters can run one.
items.fix: EIP-4444 history expiry, stateless clients, and Verkle trees decouple storage from running a node.

exercise.title: Oracle Attack — Step by Step
exercise.subtitle: A crop insurance contract is hacked via its oracle. Click through each step — and see how to prevent it.

rows.consequence: Contract stores: if rainfall < 50mm in June → pay out $5,000 automatically.

rows.event: A single company runs the only weather oracle
rows.consequence: The smart contract trusts this oracle completely. It's the only source of truth for rainfall data.

mitigation.title: Decentralised Oracle (Chainlink)
mitigation.desc: Aggregate from 15+ independent nodes — attacker must compromise a majority simultaneously.

rows.event: Hacker compromises the oracle's API key
rows.consequence: The attacker can now submit any rainfall figure they want — the blockchain has no way to verify it.

mitigation.title: Multiple Data Sources
mitigation.desc: Cross-check NOAA + Weather.com + satellite data — one compromised source is caught by the others.

rows.consequence: The smart contract reads this value, sees the condition is met (10 < 50), and executes automatically.

mitigation.title: Dispute Window
mitigation.desc: Add a 24h challenge period before payout — observers can flag suspicious oracle data.

rows.consequence: The contract behaved exactly as coded. It did nothing wrong. The vulnerability was the oracle — not the contract.

mitigation.title: Multisig Oracle Control
mitigation.desc: Require 3-of-5 oracle operators to sign the data — no single point of control.

rows.consequence: The transaction is immutable. The code has already executed. There is no appeals process, no reversal.

security.heading: Security Risks
security.subtitle: Bugs are public, permanent, and exploited within hours. The most expensive ones are the simplest.

items.title: Reentrancy
items.what: Calls out before updating its own state — attacker calls back in and drains it.

items.title: Flash Loan Attacks
items.what: Borrow millions uncollateralised, bend a price oracle, exploit, repay — one atomic tx.

items.title: Access Control
items.what: Critical functions (mint, withdraw, upgrade) miss the right modifier.

items.title: Over/Underflow
items.what: Pre-0.8 Solidity wraps silently — balance − x underflows to a huge number.

items.title: Logic Bugs
items.what: Code runs as written, but the logic is wrong — off-by-one, bad sign, wrong arg.
items.stat: Tools miss these — humans catch them

items.title: Cumulative Loss
items.what: Thousands of incidents — usually a missing modifier or a copy-paste slip.
items.fix: Audits are a discipline, not a checkbox

cost.heading: Cost Risks
cost.subtitle: Smart contracts are not plug-and-play — budget for the upfront and the ongoing.

items.title: Immutability & Redeployment
items.what: You can't patch — you redeploy
items.why: A fix means deploying a new contract and migrating users + state. Ship-and-iterate does not apply.
items.fix: Heavy testing and secure design before launch; plan a v1→v2 migration path.

items.title: Development & Maintenance
items.what: Scarce, expensive expertise
items.why: Solidity is complex and fast-moving; even strong devs ship bugs, and immutability blocks normal patching.
items.fix: Budget premium rates for auditors and security engineers as a line item, not an afterthought.

items.title: Gas & Execution
items.what: Fees vary and compound
items.why: Costs spike 10–100× under congestion, and inefficient code taxes every future user forever.
items.fix: Optimise storage writes and external calls; reconsider micro-payment economics.

items.title: Security vs Performance
items.what: Every safety check costs gas
items.why: Defensive checks trade off against efficiency, and audit cost scales with code complexity.
items.fix: A ~$50K audit per ~500 lines is normal; bug bounties add cost but cut catastrophic risk.

regulatory.heading: Regulatory Risks
regulatory.subtitle: Smart contracts blur code and law — and the law has not caught up.

items.title: Legal Adjudication
items.what: May not count as a valid contract
items.body: Often no signatures or mutual assent, so court enforcement is uncertain — and "code is law" gets overruled when stakes are high (The DAO fork).
items.tag: Pair on-chain logic with a legal wrapper

items.title: Privacy vs Transparency
items.what: Public by design clashes with GDPR
items.body: Every tx is visible to all nodes — colliding with the right to be forgotten and HIPAA. Even hashes leak via metadata and linkability.
items.tag: ZK proofs · permissioned chains · off-chain encryption

items.title: Tech Outpaces Law
items.what: Rules lag the technology by years
items.body: Use cases shift in months while frameworks take years; a contract legal in Singapore may be illegal in the US.
items.tag: EU's MiCA (2024) is the first comprehensive exception

quiz.question: A DeFi lending protocol uses a single on-chain DEX spot price as its collateral oracle. An attacker takes a $50M flash loan, manipulates the DEX price, borrows against inflated collateral, and repays the loan — all in one transaction. Which property of flash loans makes this attack unique compared to a standard market manipulation?
options: Flash loans are anonymous — the attacker cannot be traced or prosecuted.
Flash loans require no capital — the entire attack is atomic and self-financing within a single block, leaving no trace between blocks.
Flash loans exploit a bug in the Ethereum protocol itself, making them impossible to prevent at the contract level.
Flash loans allow attackers to bypass gas fees, making the attack economically viable even for small protocols.
quiz.explanation: Traditional market manipulation requires large capital held at risk over time. Flash loans change the economics completely: the attacker borrows an enormous amount, executes the attack, and repays everything within a single atomic transaction. If any step fails, the entire transaction reverts — the attacker risks only gas fees. This means any protocol using a single on-chain spot price is vulnerable to an adversary with virtually zero capital. The fix: time-weighted average prices (TWAP) cannot be manipulated within a single block, and decentralized oracle networks aggregate from multiple independent sources.

takeaways.title: Section 05 — Key Takeaways
items: The oracle problem: smart contracts cannot access external data trustlessly — bridges introduce new attack surfaces
Immutability is a double-edged sword — bugs are permanent unless upgrade patterns are used
"Code is law" means users have no recourse when code behaves correctly but harmfully
Gas costs make complex on-chain logic economically impractical for many use cases
Smart contracts are not always the answer — a database is faster, cheaper, and easier in many contexts
Security audits are not optional — $6B+ lost since 2016; treat smart contract security as a discipline
Regulatory uncertainty: laws move in years, contracts move in months — privacy, enforceability, and jurisdiction all matter

summary.heading: Section Summary
summary.subtitle: Limitations and risks — at a glance

cards.title: Reentrancy
cards.summary: Attacker re-enters function before state updates — The DAO hack ($60M, 2016). Fix: Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern

cards.title: Oracle Attacks
cards.summary: Flash loan → manipulate AMM price → drain protocol — Harvest Finance ($34M, 2020). Fix: multi-oracle, TWAP

cards.title: Gas & Scale
cards.summary: Computation is metered and expensive on L1 — L2 rollups (Arbitrum, Optimism) reduce cost 10–100×

cards.title: Legal & Regulatory
cards.summary: Enforceability uncertain · GDPR conflicts · jurisdictional differences · "code is law" rejected by courts

cards.title: Cost Realities
cards.summary: Audit costs · gas optimisation discipline · maintenance is expensive · migrations are projects

cards.title: Mitigations
cards.summary: Audits + formal verification · multi-oracle design · timelocks · upgrade proxy patterns · bug bounties
